{{AI-stub}}
{{Infobox proglang
|name = Zymbol-Lang
|paradigms = [[Imperative]], [[Functional]], [[Procedural]]
|author = [[User:Zymbol.Lang]]
|year = 2026
|typesystem = [[Dynamic typing|Dynamic]]
|memsystem = Automatic (Rust-managed)
|class = [[Turing complete]]
|majorimpl = [https://github.com/zymbol-lang/interpreter/releases/tag/v0.0.5 v0.0.5] ([https://github.com/zymbol-lang/interpreter source], Rust)
|dialects = None
|influence = [[Haskell]], [[Ruby]], [[Rust (programming language)|Rust]]
|files = .zy
}}

'''Zymbol-Lang''' is a programming language designed to be completely agnostic to which natural language programs are written in — achieved by replacing every keyword with symbolic punctuation sequences, so that no human language's vocabulary is privileged over another. The language embodies a single guiding principle: ''operators are universal, identifiers are yours''.

The design is intentional and rigorous, authored by [[User:Zymbol.Lang]] ([https://github.com/zymbol-lang/interpreter/commits?author=OscarEEspinozaB OscarE.EspinozaB]), who defined the language specification, philosophy, and architecture. Implementation — compiler, register VM, LSP server, VS Code extension, web playground — was carried out in [[:Category:2026|2026]] using [[Claude Code]] as the engineering team: the AI executed the designer's detailed specifications rather than originating them. Delivering a complete toolchain of this scope as a solo project would have been economically and practically out of reach without AI assistance; Claude Code made that scale achievable for one person.

Zymbol-Lang does not aim for mass adoption. Its purpose is a proof of concept: demonstrating that a high-level, general-purpose [[esolang]] can be genuinely functional in the real world while remaining syntactically independent of any human language. Working GitHub projects — modest in scope but entirely operational — show that the idea holds in practice. The language invites curiosity: this is not AI-generated slop, but a meticulous, carefully guided design that used AI as a capable and disciplined engineering partner. Without that partnership, none of it would exist.

== Overview ==

Zymbol-Lang is currently in public alpha (v0.0.5), with two execution backends: a tree-walking interpreter (default, fully featured) and a register-based VM (<code>--vm</code>, ~4.4× faster on recursive workloads). Source files use the <code>.zy</code> extension.

Design principles:
# '''No keywords''' — <code>?</code> is if, <code>@</code> is loop, <code><~</code> is return. Nothing to translate.
# '''Full Unicode''' — identifiers, strings, and comments accept any Unicode character or script.
# '''Language-agnostic types''' — <code>#1</code>/<code>#0</code> instead of <code>true</code>/<code>false</code>.
# '''Explicit over implicit''' — newlines in output and return values require explicit syntax.
# '''1-based indexing''' — <code>arr[1]</code> is the first element; <code>arr[-1]</code> is the last.

== Syntax ==

=== Key Operators ===

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Category !! Operator !! Meaning !! Notes
|-
| Assignment || <code>=</code> / <code>:=</code> || Mutable variable / immutable constant || Reassigning <code>:=</code> is a runtime error
|-
| Output || <code>>></code> || Print (no auto-newline) || <code>¶</code> or <code>\\</code> for explicit newline
|-
| Input || <code><<</code> || Read from stdin || Accepts optional prompt string
|-
| If / Else-if / Else || <code>?</code> / <code>_?</code> / <code>_</code> || Conditional branches || Braces required even for single statements
|-
| Match || <code>??</code> || Pattern match / switch || Arms: <code>pattern => result</code>; literal, range, comparison, wildcard
|-
| Loop || <code>@</code> || Range, for-each, times, or infinite || <code>i:0..N</code>; step: <code>i:0..N:S</code>; times: <code>@ N {}</code>
|-
| Break / Continue || <code>@!</code> / <code>@></code> || Exit or advance loop || Labeled: <code>@:name!</code> / <code>@:name></code>
|-
| Return || <code><~</code> || Return value from function || Also declares output parameters in signatures
|-
| Lambda || <code>-></code> || Anonymous function || Always captures outer scope (closure)
|-
| Pipe || <code>\|></code> || Pipe value into function || <code>_</code> marks the injection point
|-
| Collections || <code>$#</code> <code>$+</code> <code>$-</code> <code>$></code> <code>$\|</code> <code>$<</code> || Length, append, remove, map, filter, reduce ||
|-
| Error handling || <code>!?{ }</code> / <code>:! ##Type</code> / <code>:></code> || Try / catch / finally || Error types: <code>##IO</code>, <code>##Parse</code>, <code>##Index</code>, <code>##Type</code>…
|-
| Modules || <code><#</code> / <code>#></code> / <code>::</code> || Import / export / call || Alias required: <code><# ./lib/m => m</code>
|-
| Numeral mode || <code>#d0d9#</code> || Switch numeric output digit script || 69 Unicode scripts; <code>#०९#</code> = Devanagari
|}

=== Types ===

Types are dynamic. The postfix <code>#?</code> operator returns a structured tuple <code>(type_tag, size, value)</code>.

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Type !! Example !! Tag !! Notes
|-
| Integer || <code>42</code>, <code>0xFF</code> || <code>###</code> || 64-bit signed; any Unicode digit script in source
|-
| Float || <code>3.14</code>, <code>1.5e10</code> || <code>##.</code> || Integer ÷ integer = integer
|-
| String || <code>"hello {name}"</code> || <code>##"</code> || Interpolation with <code>{}</code>
|-
| Bool || <code>#1</code>, <code>#0</code> || <code>##?</code> || Not numeric; adapts to active numeral script
|-
| Array || <code>[1, 2, 3]</code> || <code>##]</code> || 1-indexed; <code>arr[-1]</code> = last element
|-
| Tuple || <code>(x: 1, y: 2)</code> || <code>##)</code> || Immutable; named fields via <code>.field</code>
|-
| Lambda || <code>x -> x * 2</code> || <code>##-></code> || First-class; always captures enclosing scope
|}

Named functions are first-class values since v0.0.4. Variables prefixed with <code>_</code> have exact block scope. Named functions called directly have isolated scopes; they affect the caller's state only via output parameters (<code><~</code> in the signature).

== Examples ==

=== Hello, World! ===

<pre>
>> "Hello, World!" ¶
</pre>

In Arabic (right-to-left identifiers, same operators):

<pre>
تحية = "مرحبا، أيها العالم!"
>> تحية ¶
</pre>

=== Control Flow and Loops ===

<pre>
x = 7
? x > 100 { >> "large" ¶ }
_? x > 0  { >> "positive" ¶ }
_         { >> "zero or negative" ¶ }

@ i:1..5      { >> i " " }      // → 1 2 3 4 5
@ item:["apple", "mango"] { >> item ¶ }
</pre>

=== Functions and Higher-Order Functions ===

<pre>
add(a, b) { <~ a + b }
double = x -> x * 2

nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
>> nums$> double ¶                          // map  → [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]
>> nums$| (x -> x % 2 == 0) ¶              // filter → [2, 4]
>> nums$< (0, (acc, x) -> acc + x) ¶       // reduce → 15
</pre>

=== FizzBuzz ===

<pre>
fizzbuzz(n) {
    ? n % 15 == 0 { <~ "FizzBuzz" }
    _? n % 3  == 0 { <~ "Fizz" }
    _? n % 5  == 0 { <~ "Buzz" }
    _ { <~ n }
}
@ i:1..20 { >> fizzbuzz(i) ¶ }
</pre>

=== Modules ===

<pre>
// lib/math.zy
# math {
    #> { add }
    add(a, b) { <~ a + b }
}

// main.zy
<# ./lib/math => m
>> m::add(5, 3) ¶    // → 8
</pre>

== Internationalization ==

Because Zymbol-Lang has no English keywords, programs written in any human language are syntactically identical — only identifiers, string contents, and comments change. Numeral modes extend this to numeric output: <code>#०९#</code> switches all number display to Devanagari digits; 69 Unicode scripts are supported, including Klingon pIqaD (U+F8F0–U+F8F9). Native digit literals from any supported script may appear directly in source code.

== Implementation ==

Written in [[Rust (programming language)|Rust]]. Pipeline: Lexer → Parser → Semantic Analysis → Tree-Walker (default) or Bytecode Compiler → Register VM (<code>--vm</code>).

<pre>
zymbol run program.zy          # tree-walker (default)
zymbol run --vm program.zy     # register VM
zymbol repl                    # interactive REPL
zymbol check program.zy        # syntax and semantic check
</pre>

== Tooling ==

Beyond the core interpreter, Zymbol-Lang ships a set of official tools:

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Tool !! Description !! Links
|-
| '''VS Code Extension''' || Syntax highlighting, LSP integration (autocomplete, diagnostics, go-to-definition), snippets, formatter, and in-editor runner. Connects to the same <code>zymbol-lsp</code> server included in the interpreter release. || [https://github.com/zymbol-lang/vscode Source (GitHub)]
|-
| '''Web Playground''' || Browser-based interactive environment running a JavaScript mirror of the tree-walker interpreter. Supports multi-file projects, i18n examples, and 69 numeral scripts — no installation required. || [https://zymbol-lang.org/playground.html Try it online] · [https://github.com/zymbol-lang/web Source (GitHub)]
|-
| '''Installer''' || Platform packages and installation instructions for Linux, macOS, and Windows. || [https://zymbol-lang.org/install.html Install page]
|}

== See Also ==

* [[Brainfuck]] — another language with a minimal operator set
* [[Befunge]] — symbolic, stack-based esolang
* [[Unlambda]] — purely functional with minimal syntax

== External Links ==

* [https://zymbol-lang.org Official website]
* [https://github.com/zymbol-lang/interpreter Source code (GitHub)]
* [https://github.com/zymbol-lang/interpreter/blob/main/zymbol.ebnf Formal EBNF grammar]

[[Category:Languages]]
[[Category:2026]]
[[Category:Implemented]]
[[Category:Turing complete]]
[[Category:Functional]]
[[Category:Imperative]]
[[Category:Dynamic typing]]
[[Category:Rust]]
[[Category:Unicode]]
[[Category:Generated by AI]]
